elements.sent-2411-001: This paper proposes a two-phase text-to-floorplan generation method, which guides a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate an initial layout (Layout-LLM) and refines them into the final floorplans through conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-002: We incorporate a Chain-of-Thought approach to prompt the LLM based on user text specifications, enabling a more user-friendly and intuitive house layout design.
elements.sent-2411-003: This method allows users to describe their needs in natural language, enhancing accessibility and providing clearer geometric constraints.
elements.sent-2411-004: The final floorplans generated by Layout-LLM through conditional diffusion refinement are more accurate and better meet user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across all metrics, validating its effectiveness in practical home design applications.
elements.sent-2411-006: We plan to release our code for public use.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-CoT.pdf} \caption{Comparison of different floorplan generation paradigms. (a) Graph to house layout approach (e.g., HouseDiffusion), where rooms are represented as nodes and doors as edges, forming a graph that represents spatial relationships; (b) Text-to-floorplan approach, which directly maps a natural language description to a house layout; (c) Our two-phase paradigm, where an LLM is used to generate an initial layout, Layout-LLM, according to the user's textual specification. The initial solution serves as a condition for generating the final layout, Layout-Final, through a diffusion model.} \label{fig:introduction}
elements.sent-2411-007: In architectural design, creating floorplans that meet user requirements remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-008: Traditional design methods rely not only on specialized knowledge but also require designers to make iterative adjustments to accommodate specific user needs, making personalized design difficult.
elements.sent-2411-009: Trained models~\cite{murali2017indoor,merrell2010computer,sun2022wallplan,luo2022floorplangan have made efforts to improve the accuracy, interactivity, and efficiency of floorplans generation.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite these advances, existing approaches have yet to achieve a level of user-friendliness and accuracy that enables widespread adoption by ordinary users.
elements.sent-2411-011: The latest solutions~\cite{nauata2020house, nauata2021house, shabani2023housediffusion typically treat the floorplan creation task as a conditional generation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The conditions are expressed through a bubble diagram, where rooms are represented as nodes (or ``bubbles''), and doors as edges specifying the spatial relationships between rooms, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:introduction (a).}
elements.sent-2411-013: While the bubble diagram provides a precise way of describing the house layout, specifying a consistent graph structure can still be too demanding for novice users who wish to explore designing with minimal effort.
elements.sent-2411-014: We believe that a more user-friendly interface should allow users to express their needs in natural language.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, the user could simply specify {\em ``I need a house with three bedrooms, a living room, a bathroom, a kitchen, and a balcony adjacent to the living room.'' Alternatively, the user may use a simple menu to make choices, which are then translated into natural language, perhaps in a more structured format, such as JSON.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The text-to-floorplan paradigm would make the design process more accessible and intuitive for non-expert users.
elements.sent-2411-017: A possible solution to the above requirement would be to use a generation model that directly maps text to house layouts, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:introduction (b).}
elements.sent-2411-018: This end-to-end approach, however, faces significant challenges in meeting fine-grained geometric and numeric constraints specified in the text.
elements.sent-2411-019: Successfully training such models at least requires massive datasets, which are currently unavailable for floorplans.
elements.sent-2411-020: Indeed, our experiments will confirm the ineffectiveness of this approach even with the largest floorplan datasets available to date.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address these issues, we propose a novel two-phase generation approach, called {\bf HouseLLM .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the first phase, we use a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM, or LLM for brevity) to interact with the user and translate the text to an initial design of house layout, termed Layout-LLM.
elements.sent-2411-023: In the second phase, we use a diffusion model to refine this initial design into a more accurate, final house layout, termed Layout-Final.
elements.sent-2411-024: This two-phase generation paradigm is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:introduction (c).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Drawing on the power of LLMs, our method provides a natural user interface and offers basic floorplan generation capabilities without the need for dedicated training.
elements.sent-2411-026: To leverage the LLM, we propose a prompting approach based on the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) technique \cite{wei2022chain, zhang2022automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-027: This approach enables generation of a house layout that meets the user's core requirements regarding the number of rooms, room types, and approximate positions, although it may have imperfections in object sizing and alignment.
elements.sent-2411-028: To further optimize the result, we have designed a diffusion model that refines the initial layout into a more accurate and detailed final floorplan.
elements.sent-2411-029: The model basically follows the design of HouseDiffusion, with a key difference: while HouseDiffusion applies conditioning only in the reverse process, our approach incorporates conditioning in both the forward (noise addition) and reverse processes, allowing for more robust alignment with the specified conditions.
elements.sent-2411-030: We have validated our approach on the RPlan dataset, in comparison with state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-031: For instance, in comparison with HouseDiffusion~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion , our method achieves the best performance across all metrics, and in particular, with a 28\% improvement in diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-032: To summarize, this paper makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We present a novel two-phase floorplan generation approach that leverages LLM to greatly reduce the burden of learning from scratch as adopted by previous work.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose prompt designs based on the chain-of-thought technique.
elements.sent-2411-035: The prompts successfully guide the LLM to generate initial house layouts.
elements.sent-2411-036: We design a conditional diffusion model to refine the layouts generated by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-037: The experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-intro.pdf} \caption{Training and testing processes of our method. (a) An example of LLM generating a Layout-LLM according to user demands. (b) Given a house layout sample, we use the LLM to describe it. The description is used to mimic the user's demands. Using multiple examples as in (a) as demos, we ask the LLM to generate a Layout-LMM for each sample. These initial layouts serve as conditions of the generator, which outputs Layouts-Finals. (c) Given a textual description from the user, we again use the demos like (a) to obtain a Layout-LLM, which goes through the diffusion model to generate Layout-Final.} \label{fig:LLM-intro}
elements.sent-2411-038: In the field of building design, generating high-quality house layouts has been an important research direction~\cite{hendrikx2013procedural, wu2018building, hu2020graph2plan,muller2006procedural,peng2014computing,sun2022wallplan , and many innovative approaches have emerged in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Nauata et al.~\cite{nauata2020house proposed House-GAN, a method based on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to generate house layouts.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This method achieves end-to-end automated house layout generation.
elements.sent-2411-041: Nauata et al.~\cite{nauata2021house further proposed House-GAN++, which improved the original GAN structure with enhanced generation quality.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In particular, House-GAN++ addressed the problems of missing doors, making the generated houses more practical.
elements.sent-2411-043: Upadhyay et al.~\cite{upadhyay2022flnet expanded on this by considering user inputs in the form of boundary, room types, and spatial relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Hu et al.~\cite{hu2020graph2plan presented Graph2Plan, which retrieves a set of floorplans with their associated layout graphs from a database, allowing a user to specify room counts and other layout constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Shabani et al.~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion proposed the HouseDiffusion model, which introduced bubble diagrams as constraints and used a diffusion model to generate house layouts.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Recently, Chen et al.~\cite{chen2024polydiffuse transformed visual sensor data into polygonal shapes with Diffusion Models.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Su et al.~\cite{su2024floor learn topology graphs via its bi-directional structure of ‘corruption and denoise’ is developed.. Conditional Diffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Conditional diffusion models are a subset of diffusion models~\cite{cao2024survey, sohl2015deep, yang2023diffusion where the generation is conditioned on specific input data, such as labels, images, or text, allowing more control over the output~\cite{zhang2023adding, chen2022analog, ho2022video .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Expanding on the success in diffusion model~\cite{sohl2015deep , Ho et al. proposed DDPMs~\cite{song2020denoising , introducing constraints within the diffusion process to guide generation, which led to significant improvements success.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Subsequently, conditional diffusion models DALLE-2~\cite{ramesh2022hierarchical and Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high have been applied to text-to-image generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Recent advancements continue to refine diffusion models’ conditional control.
elements.sent-2411-052: For instance, Yang et al.~\cite{yang2024improving improved diffuse image synthesis based on context prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Yang et al.~\cite{yang2024lossy proposed an end-to-end lossy image compression framework using conditional diffusion models to refine the source image.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The conditional diffusion model is a foundation of our approach, in which use it to refine the floorplan generated by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-055: HouseLLM uses a two-phase approach to generate house layouts.
elements.sent-2411-056: By breaking the generation pipeline into two phases, HouseLLM manages to exploit the power of LLMs in interpreting user demands and generating approximate layouts with their common knowledge and reasoning ability.
elements.sent-2411-057: Further refinement is designed to enhance the layout's quality.
elements.sent-2411-058: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-intro depicts the training and testing processes of our method, in which Layout-LLM plays a pivotal role.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-prompt.pdf} \caption{CoT-based prompting for generation of Layout-LLM, and its function in training and testing. (a) An example showing how to interact with the LLM to obtain Layout-LLM. The Initialization section defines the LLM's role as a house designer, and standardizes output format; the Chain of Thought section directs the LLM to create a house layout step by step, ensuring plausible room placement and sizing. (b) Invoking the Layout-LLM generation during training and testing. } \label{fig:LLM-prompt}
elements.sent-2411-059: Natural language descriptions alone may lack the detailed context necessary for accurate reasoning about specific house layouts~\cite{mann2020language, workrethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-060: To address this limitation, we enhance the LLM's reasoning capability by allowing it to reference multiple human-designed layout demos, following the Chain of Thought (CoT) approach.
elements.sent-2411-061: By demonstrating each step explicitly, including room selection, placement, and sizing decisions, CoT helps the LLM break down complex layout generation tasks into manageable, step-by-step inferences.
elements.sent-2411-062: The demoset~$E$ for the prompt engineering consists of pairs of language description~$e_d$ and corresponding Layout-LLM~$e_l$: \begin{small \begin{equation}E=\{(e_l,e_d)_1,(e_l,e_d)_2,...,(e_l,e_d)_n\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-intro (a) shows the reasoning process of an LLM generation example.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Figures~\ref{fig:LLM-intro (b), (c) illustrate the training and testing pipelines of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-065: In the testing mode, we input user prompts expressed in natural language and the demo set $E$ into the LLM to generate the initial layout.
elements.sent-2411-066: The output of the LLM is structured in JSON format, which can be described as follows: \begin{small \begin{equation}\text{House}=\{\text{rooms}=[\text{Room}_\text{1},..., \text{Room}_\text{n}]\} \label{Formula:json_tree} \end{equation} \end{small \begin{small \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{Room}_\text{i} = \{ \text{name}=[\text{Name}_\text{i}], \text{style}=\text{``modern''}, \text{position}=[(\text{X}_\text{i}, \text{Y}_\text{i})], \text{size}=[(\text{Width}_\text{i}, \text{Height}_\text{i})], \text{door}=[\text{Direction}_\text{i}] \} \end{aligned} \label{subsec:llm-format}\end{equation} \end{small where $\text{Direction _i \in \{\text{``up'' ,\text{``down'' ,\text{``left'' ,\text{``right''}\}$ The training process requires establishing a mapping from ground-truth layouts to Layout-LLMs, which are further used as conditions for generating Layout-Finals.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} Room_i = \{ name=[Name_i], style=``modern'', position=[(X_i, Y_i)], size=[(Width_i, Height_i)], door=[Direction_i] \}  \label{subsec:llm-format}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: Given a house layout sample from the dataset, we first use the LLM to give a description of the layout, which mimics the user's demands.
elements.sent-2411-068: The description is then fed to the LLM, which generates Layout-LLMs using the CoT-based prompt.
elements.sent-2411-069: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-prompt shows a sample of prompts design.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The prompt used in our method consists of three parts: Initialization, Chain of Thought (CoT), and Layout-LLM generation.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/network.pdf} \caption{Conditional diffusion network for refining Layout-LLM. The forward process takes the ground-truth house layout $x^g_0$ and the Layout-LLM $x^l_0$ and adds a Gaussian noise to create a noisy house layout sample $x_T$. The reverse process takes a noisy house layout at time $t$ with Layout-LLM as the condition. Two encoders are used to encode and obtain the latent representations for $x^g_t$ and $x^l_t$. } \label{fig:network}
elements.sent-2411-071: Following HouseDiffusion~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion , our conditional generation method is inspired by the diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The reverse process consists of a set of modules: Layout-LLM Encoder, Noisy Encoder, Continuous Decoder and Discrete Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-073: Figure~\ref{fig:network presents the specific training method of our model.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Unlike HouseDiffusion~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion , which applies conditional information only during noise decoding, our approach fuses conditional information in both noise addition and decoding phases.}
elements.sent-2411-075: By embedding Layout-LLM from the start, the model builds condition-relevant features progressively during noise accumulation and continues to refine them in the denoising phase.
elements.sent-2411-076: This dual-phase conditioning enhances condition alignment and improves the stability and accuracy of the generated layouts.
elements.sent-2411-077: Diffusion models progressively refine Gaussian noise \begin{small}$x_T$\end{small over \begin{small}$T$\end{small , guided by a conditional Layout-LLM \begin{small}$y$\end{small .}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The forward process takes an initial data sample \begin{small}$x_0$\end{small , a conditional Layout-LLM \begin{small}$y$\end{small and generates a noisy sample \begin{small}$x_t$\end{small at time step \begin{small}$t$\end{small by sampling Gaussian noise \begin{small}$\epsilon \sim N(0,1)$\end{small , incorporating information from the conditional layout through its embedding \begin{small}$e(y)$\end{small : \begin{small \begin{equation}x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}*x_0+\sqrt{1-\alpha_t}*\epsilon+e(y)\end{equation} \end{small $\alpha_t$ represents the noise schedule, controlling noise intensity changes from $1$ to $0$ at each time step $t$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The reverse denoising process starts with a fully noise-added sample $x_T$ and gradually removes noise to produce a sample that matches the target data distribution $x_0$.
elements.sent-2411-080: This reverse process is typically implemented as an iterative process, where each time step depends on the output of the previous step and incorporates conditional information $y$ to guide the generation.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the reverse denoising process at time step $t$, the model $f_{\theta}$ predicts the denoised image $\hat{x _0$: \begin{small \begin{equation}\hat{x}_0 = f_{\theta}(x_t,t,e(y))\end{equation} \end{small $f_{\theta}$ is the denoising network Transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention parameterized by $\theta$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The reverse process updates the state of $x_t$ each step to remove noise and gradually guide the generated image toward the structure of the condition $y$.
elements.sent-2411-083: By iterating the update formula from time step $T$ to $1$, the reverse process progressively removes noise, bringing $x_t$ closer to a clean sample $x_0$.
elements.sent-2411-084: At each time step, the embedding $e(y)$ from the condition $y$ continues to guide the generation, ensuring that the final output conforms to the structure and characteristics of the conditional layout.
elements.sent-2411-085: Here we refer to the processing of data representation in~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion to meet the constraints on the number and type of rooms.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Suppose \begin{small}$P^g=\{P^g_1,P^g_2,...,P^g_N\}$\end{small is the set of polygonal loops for each room/door in the original data set.}
elements.sent-2411-087: For \begin{small}$\forall P \in \{P^g,P^{l ,P^f\}$\end{small , each $p_i$ is defined by a sequence of corners with as a 2-dim coordinates: \begin{small \begin{equation}P_i=\{C_{i,1},C_{i,2},...,C_{i,N_i}|C_{i,j}\in R^2\}\end{equation} \end{small Latent Encoder.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Given a floorplan sample \begin{small}$P^g_t$\end{small and corresponding Layout-LLM \begin{small}$P^l_t$\end{small at time $t$, every room/door corner \begin{small}$C_{i,j ^t$\end{small is mapped to \begin{small}$\hat{C ^t_{i,j}$\end{small through the Layout-LLM and the Noisy encoder: \begin{small \begin{equation}\label{equ:linear_forward} Linear([AU(C^g_{i,j})_{t},AU(C^{l}_{i,j})_{t},R_i,\mathbf{1}(i),\mathbf{1}(j),t])\end{equation} \end{small Following the method in \cite{shabani2023housediffusion , AU enhances relational inference by inserting angular coordinates.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: The scalar $t$ is included, and a linear layer transforms the embedding into a 512-dimensional vector.. Continuous Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our reverse process architecture incorporates a sequence of attention layers with structured masking to process embedding vectors \begin{small}$\hat{C ^t_{i,j}$\end{small .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Each embedding vector \begin{small}$\hat{C ^t_{i,j}$\end{small undergoes attention layers, which consist of three distinct types of attention: Component-wise Self Attention (CSA), Global Self Attention (GSA) and Relational Cross Attention (RCA).}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Following the attention layers, a final linear layer is used to infer noise \begin{small}$\epsilon (C_{i,j ,t)$\end{small , aiding in the iterative refinement of coordinates.. Discrete Decoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Our approach aims to capture geometric relationships (such as collinearity, orthogonality, or corner adjacency), which are difficult to maintain through continuous coordinate regression alone.
elements.sent-2411-094: As in \cite{shabani2023housediffusion , we infer coordinates in discrete form.}
elements.sent-2411-095: After obtaining \begin{small}$C^{t-1 _{i,j}$\end{small and \begin{small}$C^0_{i,j}$\end{small from the denoising process, we map \begin{small}$C^0_{i,j}$\end{small back to the range [0,255] through an affine transformation, round the values, and convert them to binary representation using an “int2bit” function, resulting in an 8-dimensional binary vector.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: For each corner, we apply a linear transformation to generate a 512-dimensional embedding vector: \begin{small \begin{equation}\label{equ:discrete} Linear([AU(C^g_{i,j})_{t},AU(C^{l}_{i,j})_t,R_i,\mathbf{1}(i),\mathbf{1}(j),t])\end{equation} \end{small To enhance network performance, we pass \begin{small}$C^0_{i,j}$\end{small as the input and use the binary representation from the int2bit function along with conditions, thus providing an initial discrete representation to guide learning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: We then apply two additional blocks of attention with structured masking, followed by a final linear layer to produce the 8-dimensional \begin{small}$C^0_{i,j}$\end{small vector.}
elements.sent-2411-098: During testing, we apply binary thresholding to \begin{small}$C^0_{i,j}$\end{small to retrieve integer coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-099: We train our model with the simple L2-norm regression loss by~\cite{ho2020denoising .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The loss function consists of noise prediction loss and corner coordinate regression loss.
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, \begin{small}$\epsilon_\theta (C_{i,j ,t)$\end{small is compared with the ground-truth noise to quantify the discrepancy between them.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The discrepancy is typically measured using the masked mean squared error as follows: \begin{small \begin{equation}Loss_{n} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^N \Vert \epsilon_\theta(C_{i,j}, t) - \epsilon \Vert_2}{N}\end{equation} \end{small where $N$ represents total number of samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Therefore, the discrete branch is used during training only when \begin{small}$C_{i,j}$\end{small .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \abovetopsep=0pt \aboverulesep=0pt \belowrulesep=0pt \belowbottomsep=0pt \caption{Generation performance of different methods. } \label{table:all_1} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{c |c |c c c c| c c c c} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} Realism ($\uparrow$) \multicolumn{4}{c|} {Diversity ($\downarrow$)} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Compatibility ($\downarrow$)} 6 5 6 7 8 5 6 7 8 House-GAN\cite{nauata2020house} -0.95 37.5 41.0 32.9 66.4 2.5 2.4 3.2 5.3 House-GAN++\cite{nauata2021house} -0.52 30.4 37.6 27.3 32.9 1.9 2.2 2.4 3.9 HouseDiffusion\cite{shabani2023housediffusion} -0.19 11.2 10.3 10.4 9.5 1.5 1.2 1.7 2.5 PuzzleFusion\cite{hossieni2024puzzlefusion} - \multicolumn{4}{c|}{10.55} \multicolumn{4}{c}{0.97} \cellcolor{Gray}{Ours} \cellcolor{Gray}{-0.03} \cellcolor{Gray}{8.6} \cellcolor{Gray}{7.5} \cellcolor{Gray}{8.1} \cellcolor{Gray}{9.0} \cellcolor{Gray}{0.24} \cellcolor{Gray}{0.25} \cellcolor{Gray}{0.28} \cellcolor{Gray}{0.32} \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=.9\linewidth]{figures/result1.pdf} \caption{Generation samples from HouseLLM and HouseDiffusion. The results of HouseLLM align well with user requirements (as translated from the of original data samples) in the number and type of rooms, also in the overall room distribution. HouseDiffusion generally performs well, but in some cases, it can generate gaps or holes within the house (indicated by the dashed-line rectangles), along with misaligned object placement, which make the layout unrealistic.} \label{fig:result1}
elements.sent-2411-104: Our method is implemented in PyTorch and and accelerated using an NVIDIA Quadro RTX 8000.
elements.sent-2411-105: The batch size is set to 128 and learning rate is 1e-3.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our models are optimized by Adam~\cite{kingma2014adam with weight decay~\cite{loshchilov2017decoupled for 250K steps and the initial learning rate is 1e-3.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The network architecture can be found in Appendix.. Datasets.
elements.sent-2411-108: We use the commonly used public dataset RPlan~\cite{wu2019data for experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-109: RPlan represents the largest dataset for house plan with over 80K images.
elements.sent-2411-110: We divide RPlan into five groups (5, 6, 7, 8 rooms) according to the number of rooms for cross validation.
elements.sent-2411-111: Ablation experiments were conducted on the 6-rooms.
elements.sent-2411-112: To create paired groups for training, we extracted 10K initial solutions from the LLM using the prompting method from Section~\ref{sec:initialsolutiofromllm .}
elements.sent-2411-113: GPT-4o was used as the LLM in our approach.. Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-114: Following the evaluation method used in the prior work~\cite{nauata2020house , we employ Realism, FID and Compatibility as evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Realism is an estimate based on a user survey, considering the user's subjective experience.
elements.sent-2411-116: Diversity is measured by the FID score, defined as the Fréchet Inception Distance\cite{heusel2017gans between the two Gaussian distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-117: It is used to measure the similarity between the generated image and the real image.
elements.sent-2411-118: The Compatibility score\cite{abu2015exact is based on a graph editing distance between the layout sample and the Layout-Final.}
elements.sent-2411-119: It is used to measure the distance between generated layout and the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-121: For the existing methods, we reproduced the results reported in their respective papers.
elements.sent-2411-122: It can be seen that our method achieves superior performance in all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-123: For instance, compared with HouseDiffusion, HouseLLM brings an average improvement of 28\% in diversity and 79\% in compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-124: The significant improvements indicate that it better meets user needs in terms of flexibility in layout generation and spatial utilization efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-125: For the realism score, we followed the same process as House-GAN++ to obtain the score.
elements.sent-2411-126: We created 1000 floorplan samples for our method and showed participants two samples from different methods, asking them to select "A is better," "B is better," or "Both are equally good." Method A is awarded +1, -1, or 0 points according to the participants' choices.
elements.sent-2411-127: Each pair of methods is evaluated 150 times by 10 participants (i.e., 15 evaluations per participant).
elements.sent-2411-128: The survey results showed that 65\% of participants believed that our results were as good as or better than ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-129: Table~\ref{table:all_1 shows that HouseLLM achieves significant improvements compared to other methods, with an excellent score of -0.03, suggesting that the house layouts generated by our method are visually close to realistic layouts and meet user expectations.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Figure~\ref{fig:result1 presents a visual comparison of the outputs from HouseLLM and HouseDiffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-131: HouseLLM generates results that well align with the original data samples in the number and type of rooms.
elements.sent-2411-132: While HouseDiffusion generally performs well, it can generate layouts with misalignment and incorrect object placement.
elements.sent-2411-133: The primary reason is that with HouseDiffusion, the generation conditions are applied only during the denoising stage, restricting the model’s ability to accurately realize the spatial relationships between rooms throughout the generation (see more details in our ablation study).
elements.sent-2411-134: Furthermore, while the bubble diagram based conditions convey basic connectivity between rooms, they lack the granularity necessary to guide detailed spatial arrangements, such as room size and precise positioning.
elements.sent-2411-135: In contrast, HouseLLM combines initial layouts generated by LLM with a diffusion model that applies conditions during both noise addition and denoising stages, producing more practical house layouts.
elements.sent-2411-136: More implementation details can be found in Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-138: We compare our method with two alternative methods: the one-phase Test-to-Layout method that maps text directly to layout, and using LLM only to generate the results, that is, Layout-LLM.
elements.sent-2411-139: Figure~\ref{fig:result2 presents the comparison results of these methods under the same generation conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-140: From the figure, we can see that the results differ greatly, and with the one-phase method, text description is not enough to support the model to maintain sufficient geometric constraints.
elements.sent-2411-141: In addition, we evaluate the primary quantitative results for the one-phase and two-Phase methods.
elements.sent-2411-142: Table~\ref{table:ablation_1 presents the main quantitative evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Diversity indicates that the distribution of Text-to-Layout method result differs significantly from RPlan.
elements.sent-2411-144: This is due to a mismatch between the text dataset complexity and task, requiring extensive data support.

elements.sent-2411-151: We investigated the impact of different conditional participation ratios on the model’s generation results, as shown in Table~\ref{table:ablation_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-152: The results indicate that as the conditional ratio increases, the model performance gradually decreases, suggesting that an excessive amount of conditional information causes the model to deviate from the true distribution during generation.
elements.sent-2411-153: This may be due to the high ratio of conditional guidance, which makes the model overly dependent on the conditional information, thus weakening its ability to learn the original data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, properly controlling the conditional ratio can maintain the accuracy of generated samples.. Forward v.s.
elements.sent-2411-156: Different from the diffusion model used in HouseDiffusion, we incorporated conditional constraints in both the forward and reverse processes in our design.
elements.sent-2411-157: To assess this approach, we analyzed the impact of each constraint by varying their proportions independently.
elements.sent-2411-158: As shown in Table~\ref{table:ablation_3 , adding conditional information during the noise addition phase yields better results than adding it only during the denoising phase, as it enables the model to build condition-related features earlier.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Single-stage conditioning approach may lead to weaker constraints, resulting in inconsistent room sizes and irregular spatial relationships.
elements.sent-2411-160: The best performance is achieved when conditions are added in both phases, validating the effectiveness of the dual-phase approach.
elements.sent-2411-161: This strategy significantly enhances both accuracy and consistency, resulting in more precise and aligned outputs.
elements.sent-2411-162: This paper proposes a two-Phase Text-to-Floorplan generation model, enabling user-friendly house layout generation.
elements.sent-2411-163: This work guides the reasoning of the LLM through chain-of-thought prompting to generate an initial layout based on user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-164: The initial layout is then refined using a diffusion model to produce the final house layout.
elements.sent-2411-165: Experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-166: Given the potential of LLMs, we expect that our solution be extended to the generation of other complex architectural designs such as shopping malls and office buildings.
elements.sent-2411-167: This will be a subject of our future study.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: While the bubble diagram provides a precise way of describing the house layout, specifying a consistent graph structure~\cite{zhuo2024partitioning,zhuo2022efficient,zhuo2022proximity can still be too demanding for novice users who wish to explore designing with minimal effort.}
elements.sent-2411-169: This paper proposes a two-stage text-to-floorplan generation framework that combines the reasoning capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) with the generative power of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-170: In the first stage, we leverage a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting strategy to guide an LLM in generating an initial layout (Layout-Init) from natural language descriptions, which ensures a user-friendly and intuitive design process.
elements.sent-2411-171: However, Layout-Init may lack precise geometric alignment and fine-grained structural details.
elements.sent-2411-172: To address this, the second stage employs a conditional diffusion model to refine Layout-Init into a final floorplan (Layout-Final) that better adheres to physical constraints and user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-173: Unlike prior methods, our approach effectively reduces the difficulty of floorplan generation learning without the need for extensive domain-specific training data.
elements.sent-2411-174: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across all metrics, which validates its effectiveness in practical home design applications.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our code will be made publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-176: In architectural design, creating floorplans that align with user requirements remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-177: Traditional design methods not only rely on specialized knowledge but also require designers to make iterative adjustments to accommodate specific user needs, which makes personalized design challenging.
elements.sent-2411-178: Learning-based models~\cite{murali2017indoor,merrell2010computer,sun2022wallplan,luo2022floorplangan have made efforts to improve the accuracy, interactivity, and efficiency of floorplan generation.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Despite these advances, existing approaches have yet to achieve a level of user-friendliness and accuracy that enables ready adoption by ordinary users.
elements.sent-2411-180: Existing solutions~\cite{nauata2020house, nauata2021house, shabani2023housediffusion typically treat the floorplan creation task as a conditional generation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-181: For example, the user could simply specify {\em ``I need a house with three bedrooms, a living room, a bathroom, a kitchen, and a balcony adjacent to the living room.'' Alternatively, the user may use a simple menu to make choices, which are then translated into natural language.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The text-to-floorplan paradigm make the design process more accessible and intuitive for non-expert users.
elements.sent-2411-183: A solution to this requirement involves training a generative model capable of directly mapping textual descriptions to house layouts, as depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:introduction (b).}
elements.sent-2411-184: The Tell2Design method~\cite{leng2023Tell2Design addresses this task using a Sequence-to-Sequence approach, where the input text specifies the floorplan boundary and the exact geometry of individual rooms.}
elements.sent-2411-185: The method imposes stringent demands for geometric detail and consistency, placing an even greater burden on users compared to working with bubble diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-186: In addition, the design limits the diversity of the results, which is often crucial for exploratory design.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-intro.pdf} \caption{Comparison of different floorplan generation pipelines. (a) Graph-to-floorplan approach (e.g., HouseDiffusion), where rooms are represented as nodes and doors as edges, forming a graph~\cite{zhuo2022efficient, zhuo2022proximity, zhuo2024partitioning} that represents spatial relationships; (b) Text-to-floorplan approach, which directly maps a natural language description to a house layout; (c) Our two-stage pipeline, where an LLM is used to generate an initial layout, Layout-Init, according to the user's textual specification. The initial solution serves as a condition for generating the final layout, Layou-Final, through a diffusion model.} \label{fig:introduction}
elements.sent-2411-187: We introduce \ours, a novel two-stage floorplan generation framework.
elements.sent-2411-188: In the first stage, we use a multimodal large language model (LLM) to generate an initial house layout, termed {\bf Layout-Init .}
elements.sent-2411-189: The LLM’s common-sense knowledge and reasoning capability enable the creation of a preliminary floorplan based on user input.
elements.sent-2411-190: In the second stage, a diffusion model refines this initial design into a more precise and reasonable final layout, referred to as {\bf Layout-Final .}
elements.sent-2411-191: This two-stage generation process is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:introduction (c).}
elements.sent-2411-192: Generating a reasonable house layout using an LLM is not straightforward, as LLMs often struggle to interpret exact numeric and geometric constraints.
elements.sent-2411-193: To address this, we employ a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting strategy~\cite{wei2022chain, zhang2022automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-194: This approach ensures the generated layout aligns with the user’s core requirements, such as the number of rooms, room types, and approximate spatial arrangements.
elements.sent-2411-195: The initial layouts produced at this stage typically exhibit imperfections in object sizing and alignment, due to the inherent limitations of LLMs in handling intricate geometric details.
elements.sent-2411-196: To overcome this, we design a diffusion model that refines the initial layout into a final floorplan.
elements.sent-2411-197: While inspired by HouseDiffusion~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion , our approach differs in its conditioning strategy: whereas HouseDiffusion applies conditioning solely during the reverse process, our model integrates conditioning in both the forward (noise addition) and reverse processes.}
elements.sent-2411-198: This dual-conditioning mechanism ensures enhanced alignment with the specified constraints, yielding more precise and robust results.
elements.sent-2411-199: We validate our approach on the RPlan dataset, in comparison with state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-200: For instance, in comparison with HouseDiffusion, our method achieves the best performance across all metrics, and in particular, with a 28\% improvement in diversity.
elements.sent-2411-201: We propose a novel two-stage floorplan generation framework that leverages the initial layouts generated by LLMs as the condition to generate the final floorplans.
elements.sent-2411-202: We develop a prompt design based on the Chain-of-Thought technique, successfully guiding the LLM to produce structured and coherent initial house layouts.
elements.sent-2411-203: We design a conditional diffusion model that refines the LLM-generated layouts into high-fidelity floorplans.
elements.sent-2411-204: Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-prompt.pdf} \caption{Training and testing processes of our method. (a) An example of LLM generating a Layout-Init according to user demands. (b) Given a house layout sample, we use the LLM to describe it. The description is used to mimic the user's demands. Using multiple examples as in (a) as demos, we ask the LLM to generate a Layout-Init for each sample. These initial layouts serve as conditions for the generator, which outputs Layouts-Final. (c) Given a textual description from the user, we again use the demos like (a) to obtain a Layout-Init, which goes through the diffusion model to generate Layout-Final.} \label{fig:LLM-intro}
elements.sent-2411-205: In the field of building design, generating high-quality house layouts has been an important research direction~\cite{hendrikx2013procedural, wu2018building, hu2020graph2plan,muller2006procedural,peng2014computing,sun2022wallplan .}
elements.sent-2411-206: Nauata et al.~\cite{nauata2020house proposed House-GAN, a method based on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) that achieves end-to-end automated house layout generation.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Nauata et al.~\cite{nauata2021house further proposed House-GAN++, which improved the original GAN structure by addressing the problems of missing doors.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Upadhyay et al.~\cite{upadhyay2022flnet expanded on this by considering user inputs in the form of boundaries, room types, and spatial relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Shabani et al.~\cite{shabani2023housediffusion introduced bubble diagrams as constraints and used a diffusion model to generate house layouts.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Chen et al.~\cite{chen2024polydiffuse transformed visual sensor data into polygonal shapes with Diffusion Models.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Su et al.~\cite{su2024floor developed a bi-directional structure of ``corruption and denoise'' approach to learn topology graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Zeng et al.~\cite{zeng2024residential proposed a multi-conditional two-stage generation model, which allows human designers to intervene and enhance controllability based on the denoising diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Leng et al.~\cite{leng2023Tell2Design introduced the Tell2Design (T2D) dataset, which comprises over 80,000 floorplan designs paired with natural language descriptions, in support of floorplan generation.}
elements.sent-2411-214: HOLODECK~\cite{yang2024holodeck explored the use of LLMs to generate floorplans populated with various objects, emphasizing the overall quality and consistency of the environments rather than dealing with complex floorplans.. Conditional Diffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Expanding on the success of diffusion models~\cite{sohl2015deep , Ho et al. proposed DDPMs~\cite{song2020denoising , introducing constraints within the diffusion process to guide generation, which led to significant significant improvements.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Subsequently, conditional diffusion models, such as DALLE-2~\cite{ramesh2022hierarchical and Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high have been applied to text-to-image generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-217: By breaking the generation pipeline into two stages, \ours\ manages to exploit the power of LLMs in interpreting user demands and generating approximate layouts with their common knowledge and reasoning ability.
elements.sent-2411-218: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-intro depicts the training and testing processes of our method, in which Layout-Init plays a pivotal role.}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/LLM-CoT.pdf} \caption{CoT-based prompting facilitates generation of Layout-Init and its function in training and testing. (a) An example showing how to interact with the LLM to obtain Layout-Init. The Initialization section defines the LLM's role as a house designer, and standardizes output format; the Chain of Thought section directs the LLM to create a house layout step by step, ensuring plausible room placement and sizing. (b) Invoking the Layout-Init generation during training and testing. } \label{fig:LLM-prompt}
elements.sent-2411-219: The demo set~$E$ for the prompt engineering consists of pairs of language description~$e_d$ and corresponding Layout-Init~$e_i$: \begin{small \begin{equation}E=\{(e_i,e_d)_1,(e_i,e_d)_2,...,(e_i,e_d)_n\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-220: The output of the LLM is structured in JSON format, which can be described as follows: \begin{small \begin{equation}\text{House}=\{\text{rooms}=[\text{Room}_\text{1},..., \text{Room}_\text{n}]\}, \label{Formula:json_tree} \end{equation} \end{small \begin{small \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{Room}_\text{i} = \{ \text{name}=[\text{Name}_\text{i}], \text{style}=\text{``modern''}, \text{position}=[(\text{X}_\text{i}, \text{Y}_\text{i})], \text{size}=[(\text{Width}_\text{i}, \text{Height}_\text{i})], \text{door}=[\text{Direction}_\text{i}] \}, \end{aligned} \label{subsec:llm-format}\end{equation} \end{small where $\text{Direction _i \in \{\text{``up'' ,\text{``down'' ,\text{``left'' ,\text{``right''}\}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation} Room_i = \{ name=[Name_i], style=``modern'', position=[(X_i, Y_i)], size=[(Width_i, Height_i)], door=[Direction_i] \},  \label{subsec:llm-format}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-221: The training process requires establishing a mapping from ground-truth layouts to Layout-Init, which are further used as conditions for generating Layout-Final.
elements.sent-2411-222: The description is then fed to the LLM, which generates Layout-Init using the CoT-based prompt.
elements.sent-2411-223: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-prompt shows a sample of prompt design.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The prompt used in our method consists of three parts: Initialization, Chain of Thought (CoT), and Layout-Init generation.. Initialization.
elements.sent-2411-225: Initialization introduces task settings, input and output format specifications, and reasoning sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-226: Specifically, the task utilizes the role-prompting technique~\cite{john2023art to instruct the LLM to play the role of a designer: we present specific tasks to the LLM, prompting it to explain, step by step, the reasoning behind the design of each house location.}
elements.sent-2411-227: An example prompt illustrating this setup is provided in see Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-prompt (a).. Chain of thought.}
elements.sent-2411-228: In each separate reasoning step, the LLM needs to determine the current house layout, then randomly select the next room to be generated, and infer the information for the next room based on the information from the existing rooms.
elements.sent-2411-229: The emphasis is on explaining the rationale behind these decisions.
elements.sent-2411-230: Figure~\ref{fig:LLM-prompt (a) presents an example that shows how to guide the model through each step of the inference process, including room selection, sizing, and positioning within the layout.. Layout-Init generation.}
elements.sent-2411-231: In this step, demos are input into the LLM to prompt the generation of the house layout.
elements.sent-2411-232: The user provides natural language input, instructs the LLM to perform a step-by-step reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-233: The final output is structured in the JSON format.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/network.pdf} \caption{Conditional diffusion network for refining Layout-Init. The forward process takes the ground-truth house layout $x^g_0$ and the Layout-Init $x^i_0$ and adds a Gaussian noise to create a noisy house layout sample $x_T$. The reverse process takes a noisy house layout at time $t$ with Layout-Init as the condition. Two encoders are used to encode and obtain the latent representations for $x^g_t$ and $x^i_t$. } \label{fig:network}
elements.sent-2411-234: The reverse process consists of a set of modules: Layout-Init Encoder, Noisy Encoder, Continuous Decoder and Discrete Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-235: By embedding Layout-Init from the start, the model builds condition-relevant features progressively during noise accumulation and continues to refine them in the denoising phase.
elements.sent-2411-236: Diffusion models progressively refine Gaussian noise \begin{small}$x_T$\end{small over \begin{small}$T$\end{small , guided by a conditional Layout-Init \begin{small}$y$\end{small .}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: The forward process takes an initial data sample \begin{small}$x_0$\end{small , a conditional Layout-Init \begin{small}$y$\end{small and generates a noisy sample \begin{small}$x_t$\end{small at time step \begin{small}$t$\end{small by sampling Gaussian noise \begin{small}$\epsilon \sim N(0,1)$\end{small , incorporating information from the conditional layout through its embedding \begin{small}$e(y)$\end{small : \begin{small \begin{equation}x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}*x_0+\sqrt{1-\alpha_t}*\epsilon+e(y).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: In the reverse denoising process at time step $t$, the model $f_{\theta}$ predicts the denoised image $\hat{x _0$: \begin{small \begin{equation}\hat{x}_0 = f_{\theta}(x_t,t,e(y)).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: The reverse process updates the state of $x_t$ each step to remove noise and gradually guide the generated image toward the structure of the condition $y$: \begin{small \begin{equation}x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}*\hat{x}_0+\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}}*\epsilon.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Suppose \begin{small}$P^G$\end{small is the set of polygonal loops for each room/door in the ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-241: For \begin{small}$\forall P \in \{P^G,P^{I ,P^F\}$\end{small , each loop $P_i$ is defined by a sequence of corners with as a 2-dim coordinates: \begin{small \begin{equation}P_i=\{C_{i,1},C_{i,2},...,C_{i,N_i}|C_{i,j}\in R^2\}.\end{equation}. \end{small Latent Encoder.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Given a floorplan sample \begin{small}$P^G_t$\end{small and corresponding Layout-Init \begin{small}$P^I_t$\end{small at time $t$, every room/door corner \begin{small}$C_{i,j ^t$\end{small is mapped to \begin{small}$\hat{C ^t_{i,j}$\end{small through the Layout-Init and the Noisy encoder: \begin{small \begin{equation}\label{equ:linear_forward} Linear([AU(C^t_{i,j})_{G},AU(C^{t}_{i,j})_{I},R_i,\mathbf{1}(i),\mathbf{1}(j),t]).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: For each corner, we apply a linear transformation to generate a 512-dimensional embedding vector: \begin{small \begin{equation}\label{equ:discrete} Linear([AU(C^0_{i,j}),AU(C^{0}_{i,j})_I,int2bit(C^{0}_{i,j}),R_i,\mathbf{1}(i),\mathbf{1}(j),t]).\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-015: \caption{Generation performance of different methods.} \label{table:all_1} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{c |c |c c c c| c c c c} Method Realism~$\uparrow$ \multicolumn{4}{c|} {Diversity~$\downarrow$} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Compatibility~$\downarrow$} Task 6 5 6 7 8 5 6 7 8 House-GAN\cite{nauata2020house} -0.95 37.5 41.0 32.9 66.4 2.5 2.4 3.2 5.3 House-GAN++\cite{nauata2021house} -0.52 30.4 37.6 27.3 32.9 1.9 2.2 2.4 3.9 HouseDiffusion\cite{shabani2023housediffusion} -0.19 11.2 10.3 10.4 9.5 1.5 1.2 1.7 2.5 PuzzleFusion\cite{hossieni2024puzzlefusion} - \multicolumn{4}{c|}{10.55} \multicolumn{4}{c}{0.97} Tell2Design~\cite{leng2023Tell2Design} -1.03 \multicolumn{4}{c|}{42.74} \multicolumn{4}{c}{-} Ours -0.03 8.6 7.5 8.1 9.0 0.24 0.25 0.28 0.32 \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-244: The discrepancy is typically measured using the masked mean squared error as follows: \begin{small \begin{equation}Loss_{n} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^N \Vert \epsilon_\theta(C_{i,j}, t) - \epsilon \Vert_2}{N},\end{equation} \end{small where $N$ represents total number of samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Our method is implemented in PyTorch and runs on an NVIDIA Quadro RTX 8000.
elements.sent-2411-246: The batch size is set to 128 and the learning rate is 1e-3.
elements.sent-2411-247: Our models are optimized by Adam~\cite{kingma2014adam with weight decay~\cite{loshchilov2017decoupled for 250K steps, with the initial learning rate is 1e-3.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The network architecture can be found in the Appendix.. Datasets.
elements.sent-2411-249: We use the public dataset RPlan~\cite{wu2019data for experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-250: RPlan represents the largest dataset for floorplan with over 80K images.
elements.sent-2411-251: We divide RPlan into five groups (5, 6, 7, 8 rooms) according to the number of rooms for cross-validation.
elements.sent-2411-252: Ablation experiments were conducted on the 6-room task.
elements.sent-2411-253: Following prior work~\cite{nauata2020house , we employ Realism, FID, and Compatibility as evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-254: It is used to measure the similarity between the generated images and the real image.
elements.sent-2411-255: It is used to measure the distance between the generated layout and the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-256: In addition, Macro IoU and Micro IoU are introduced for comparison with the Text-to-Layout method Tell2Design~\cite{leng2023Tell2Design .}
elements.sent-2411-257: Macro IoU computes the average IoU across different room types, whereas Micro IoU calculates the global IoU by aggregating the IoUs of all rooms.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/result1.pdf} \caption{Generation samples from Tell2Design, HouseDiffusion and \ours. The results of \ours\ align well with user requirements in terms of room count and type, with plausible room arrangement. The results produced by Tell2Design exhibit high similarity to the Reference layouts, demonstrating limited diversity. However, the number of generated houses deviates from the Reference, indicating inconsistencies in quantity. HouseDiffusion performs reasonably well; yet, in some cases, it generates gaps or holes within the house (indicated by the dashed-rectangles), along with misaligned object placement, which makes the layout unrealistic.} \label{fig:result1}
elements.sent-2411-258: Table~\ref{table:all_1 presents the main results, where we reproduce the reported performance of existing methods for fair comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-259: As shown in the table, our approach consistently outperforms prior methods across all evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-260: Specifically, compared to HouseDiffusion, our method improves diversity by 28\% and compatibility by 79\%, demonstrating its effectiveness in generating flexible and spatially efficient layouts.
elements.sent-2411-261: Tell2Design is evaluated using the IoU metric, with 54.34\% Micro IoU and 53.30\% Macro IoU.
elements.sent-2411-262: In comparison, \ours, when using unlabeled data, achieves a Micro IoU of 21.84\% and a Macro IoU of 17.75\%, values that are significantly lower than the results of Tell2Design with labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-263: However, due to fundamental differences in task objectives, a direct comparison based solely on IoU would be unfair.
elements.sent-2411-264: Tell2Design employs an autoregressive Seq2Seq framework, in which generation is strictly dependent on sequence order.
elements.sent-2411-265: This results in high IoU scores but significantly limits diversity (see Fig.\ref{fig:result1 (B)), as it demands a large amount of labeled data to capture layout variations.}
elements.sent-2411-266: In contrast, our non-autoregressive approach inherently mitigates these limitations, leading to a 79.88\% improvement in diversity compared to Tell2Design.
elements.sent-2411-267: Furthermore, Tell2Design relies on a costly training process involving artificial pre-training followed by fine-tuning on human annotations.
elements.sent-2411-268: In contrast, our LLM-driven method eliminates the need for manual annotation while still achieving a higher user absolute preference score~(-0.03 v.s.. -1.03).
elements.sent-2411-269: Notably, under the ``Training on Artificial Instructions Only" setting—comparable to our setup—\ours\ surpasses Tell2Design, with a Micro IoU of 15.69, compared to 9.13 for Tell2Design (+71.85\%), and a Macro IoU of 11.43, compared to 6.06 (+88.61\%).
elements.sent-2411-270: For realism evaluation, we follow the same procedure as House-GAN++ to ensure comparability.
elements.sent-2411-271: User surveys indicate that 65\% of participants perceive our generated layouts as comparable to the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-272: Implementation details are in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-273: Figure~\ref{fig:result1 presents a visual comparison of layouts generated by \ours, Tell2Design, and HouseDiffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-274: The layout descriptions for \ours, Tell2Design’s annotations, and HouseDiffusion’s graphs are all derived from the reference samples shown in Fig.\ref{fig:result1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-275: Due to the limitation of the plain Seq2Seq model, Tell2Design often produces inaccurate room counts, resulting in unrealistic layouts (see Fig.\ref{fig:result1 (B)).}
elements.sent-2411-276: In contrast, \ours\ and HouseDiffusion explicitly enforce these constraints, producing more structured and diverse layouts.
elements.sent-2411-277: HouseDiffusion, despite generally producing reasonable layouts, often exhibits misaligned and misplaced objects.
elements.sent-2411-278: This issue stems from its conditioning strategy, which applies constraints only during the denoising phase, making it less effective in maintaining spatial consistency throughout the generation process (see our ablation study for further analysis).
elements.sent-2411-279: By contrast, \ours\ refines an initial LLM-generated layout using a diffusion model with conditioning applied in both the noise addition and denoising stages.
elements.sent-2411-280: This dual-phase conditioning enhances structural coherence and spatial accuracy, leading to more practical and well-organized house layouts.
elements.sent-2411-282: We compare the one-stage Text-to-Layout approach using the text from LLM with our two-stage method.
elements.sent-2411-283: Figure~\ref{fig:result2 presents the comparison results under identical generation conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-284: As shown in the figure, the one-stage method frequently produces overlapping rooms and fails to maintain a reasonable spatial distribution.
elements.sent-2411-285: This limitation arises because textual descriptions alone do not provide sufficient geometric constraints that enable the LLM to generate well-structured layouts.
elements.sent-2411-286: Additionally, we conduct a quantitative evaluation of both the one-stage and two-stage methods.
elements.sent-2411-287: Table~\ref{table:ablation_1 presents the main quantitative evaluation results.}
elements.sent-2411-288: The details of Text-to-Layout are in the Appendix.
elements.figure-2411-017: \caption{Comparison between one-stage and two-stage methods. } \label{table:ablation_1} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{c|c c} Method Diversity~$\downarrow$ Compatibility~$\downarrow$ 1-stage: Text-to-Layout 109.5 10.6 2-stage: \ours\ 7.5 0.25 \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figures/result3.pdf} \caption{Effect of two stages in house layouts generation. (a) reference; (b) the one-stage method that maps text directly to layout; (c) LLM only, and (d) our method.} \label{fig:result2}
elements.sent-2411-289: When comparing the Layout-Init and Layout-Final, we can observe that former produces many overlapping rooms and fails to maintain a reasonable distribution; See Figure~\ref{fig:result2 .}
elements.figure-2411-019: \caption{Conditional diffusion ablation study.} \label{table:ablation_3} \begin{subtable}{0.45\textwidth} \caption{Effect of forward v.s. reverse conditioning.} \label{table:ablation_3a} \begin{tabular}{cc|cc} Forward Reverse Diversity$\downarrow$ Compatibility$\downarrow$ $\checkmark$ 8.57 24.79e-2 $\checkmark$ 14.17 24.65e-2 $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ 7.47 25.44e-2 \end{tabular} \end{subtable} \begin{subtable}{0.45\textwidth} \caption{Effect of different conditions on the generated results.} \label{table:ablation_3b} \begin{tabular}{cccc} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Rate} Macro IoU~$\uparrow$ Micro IoU~$\uparrow$ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{1e-1} 21.37\% 18.46\% \multicolumn{2}{c|}{1e-2} 20.12\% 16.59\% \multicolumn{2}{c|}{1e-3} 18.67\% 15.43\% \end{tabular} \end{subtable}
elements.sent-2411-290: Robustness and effectiveness of the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-291: Our method maintains consistent quality across both commercial and open-source LLMs, namely DeepSeek-R1 and Doubao-1.5Pro.
elements.sent-2411-292: As shown in Fig.\ref{fig:result3 and Table.\ref{table:ablation_2a , the generated JSON representations preserve structural integrity across different models, enabling seamless downstream diffusion refinement without additional fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: This demonstrates that our prompting strategy exhibits low dependency on proprietary models.
elements.sent-2411-294: Furthermore, Table~\ref{table:ablation_2b presents the ablation results for different prompt designs.}
elements.sent-2411-295: P1 employs only the task definition prompt to generate semantic representations.
elements.sent-2411-296: P2 incorporates a common-sense prompt, improving reasoning accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-297: P3 adopts a basic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompt (“Let’s think step by step”~\cite{kojima2022large ), further enhancing performance.}
elements.sent-2411-298: P4 introduces explicit reasoning chains, leading to additional improvements over P3.
elements.sent-2411-299: These results indicate that our tailored prompting strategy effectively enhances the quality of the initial layout generation.
elements.sent-2411-301: Unlike the diffusion model used in HouseDiffusion, our approach integrates conditional constraints in both the forward and reverse processes.
elements.sent-2411-302: To evaluate the effectiveness of this design, we analyzed the impact of each constraint by independently varying their proportions.
elements.sent-2411-303: As shown in Table~\ref{table:ablation_3a , incorporating conditional information during the noise addition phase leads to better performance compared to applying it only during the denoising phase.}
elements.sent-2411-304: This is because early integration of conditions allows the model to establish condition-related features at an earlier stage.
elements.sent-2411-305: A single-stage conditioning approach may result in weaker constraints, leading to errors in room sizes and spatial relationships.
elements.sent-2411-306: The best performance is achieved when conditions are applied in both phases, confirming the effectiveness of the dual-phase conditioning strategy.
elements.sent-2411-307: Furthermore, we examined the effect of different conditional participation ratios on generation quality, as presented in Table~\ref{table:ablation_3b .}
elements.sent-2411-308: The results indicate that as the conditional ratio increases, model performance gradually declines, suggesting that an excessive amount of conditional information causes the model to diverge from the true data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-309: Therefore, properly regulating the conditional ratio is crucial for maintaining the accuracy and realism of the generated samples.
elements.figure-2411-021: \caption{Prompt ablation study.} \label{table: ablation_2} \begin{subtable}{0.4\textwidth} \caption{Comparative analysis of different LLMs.} \label{table:ablation_2a} \begin{tabular}{c| c c } LLMs Diversity~$\downarrow$ IoU~$\uparrow$ DeepSeek-R1 94.3 22.3\% DouBao-1.5Pro 95.6 20.2\% GPT-4o 95.1 21.6\% \end{tabular} \end{subtable} \begin{subtable}{0.47\textwidth} \caption{Comparative analysis of different prompting strategies.} \label{table:ablation_2b} \begin{tabular}{ c | c c c } Description Diversity~$\downarrow$ IoU~$\uparrow$ P1 Task definition prompt 98.6 12.36\% P2 Common sense prompt 92.3 14.57\% P3 Think step by step 86.5 20.69\% P4 Predefined CoT prompt 75.2 21.41\% \end{tabular} \end{subtable}
elements.sent-2411-310: This paper proposes a two-stage text-to-floorplan generation model, enabling user-friendly house layout generation.
elements.sent-2411-311: This work guides the reasoning of the LLM through Chain-of-Thought prompting to generate an initial layout based on user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-312: This will be a subject of our future study.. \bibliographystyle{unsrtnat.}
